leadText: Megahorn (Japanese: メガホーン Megahorn) is a damage-dealing Bug-type move introduced in Generation II. It was TM60 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! and TR28 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. Prior to Generation III, it was the signature move of Heracross.

sections.text: Contents 1 Effect 1.1 Generation VIII 1.1.1 Pokémon Legends: Arceus 1.2 Generation IX 1.2.1 Pokémon Legends: Z-A Effect [ edit | edit source ] Megahorn deals damage and has no secondary effect. sections.title: Description
sections.text: Description [ edit | edit source ] Games Description Stad2 A Bug -type attack that uses a stiff horn or horns. It is rarely learned. G S C A powerful charge attack. R S E Colo A brutal ramming attack using out-thrust horns. FR LG A brutal ramming attack delivered with a tough and impressive horn. XD A brutal ramming attack using outthrust horns. D P Pt PBR Utilizing its tough and impressive horn, the user rams into the foe with no letup. HG SS Using its tough and impressive horn, the user rams into the foe with no letup. B W B2 W2 X Y OR AS S M US UM P E Sw Sh BD SP S V Using its tough and impressive horn, the user rams into the target with no letup. LA Using its tough and impressive horn, the user rams into the target to inflict damage. ZA The user attacks with a tough and impressive horn, ramming into targets with all-out force.

sections.title: Trivia
sections.text: Trivia [ edit | edit source ] Megahorn has the highest base power of all Bug-type moves, excluding Z-Moves , Max Moves , and G-Max Moves . A Savage Spin-Out based on Megahorn has the highest power of all Bug-type moves. In Pokémon Stadium 2 , Bugsy erroneously refers to this move as "Megaphone" when his Heracross (present only in Round 2) uses it. This is possibly due to a translation error, as ホーン can mean horn or phone (as an alternate writing of フォーン) in Japanese. This is also why this move was mistranslated as Megaphone in Korean versions prior to Generation IX . However, it should be noted that in an early prototype version of Pokémon Gold and Silver , there actually was a move called "Megaphone" (written in Japanese as 「メガホーン」) which had an associated megaphone sprite that appeared when the move was used in battle. That sprite remains unused in the code of the final versions of Pokémon Gold and Silver . This very move was, in the final versions of Generation II , replaced by Megahorn. The fact that both Megahorn and Megaphone are written identically in Japanese suggests that Game Freak decided at some point during development that it would be better to have the Bug-type Megahorn move rather than the Megaphone move, which had been for example part of the movesets of Hoothoot and Noctowl. That would mean that the name "Megahorn" itself arose out of a reanalysis of 「メガホーン」 [megahōn] ("megaphone").
